evidence: R1 Serial0/1/0 has an inbound access-group applied: ip.access-group.in.acl.acl-name = "R1_IN" (from R1 running-config).
R1 access-list "R1_IN" contains sequence 10: ace-rule action = "deny", protocol = "icmp", ipv4-address = "172.16.0.0", mask = "0.0.0.255", dst-eq-port2 = "echo" (from R1 running-config access_lists).

R1 outbound ACL to R2 (R1_TO_R2) permits ip (so the ICMP echo-request from 192.168.0.11 toward 172.16.0.12 would be forwarded), but the inbound R1_IN deny matches ICMP sourced from 172.16.0.0/24 — which would drop the echo-reply when it traverses R2->R1.

plan_history.intent_description: R1 inbound ACL R1_IN is denying ICMP from 172.16.0.0/24, causing ICMP echo-replies from 172.16.0.12 to be dropped; restore connectivity so pings from 192.168.0.11 to 172.16.0.12 succeed.
plan_history.problem: R1 inbound access-list R1_IN (applied on Serial0/1/0) contains sequence 10 denying ICMP from 172.16.0.0/24, which drops echo-replies from 172.16.0.12 and results in 0/25 pings succeeding.
plan_history.fix_summary: Remove the deny ICMP entry (sequence 10) from access-list R1_IN on router1 Serial0/1/0 to allow ICMP echo-replies from 172.16.0.12.

remaining_issues: The access-list R1_IN still exists in the running-config and contains sequence 10 denying ICMP from 172.16.0.0/24 (tool_call_id: call_JDgv6ThlxS8wMLQpGuMQmvOj). Although it's not applied inbound on Serial0/1/0 now (so traffic is passing), the ACL entry remains and could cause future issues if applied elsewhere or re-applied to the interface.
